narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 34 (under the usual 36.7), and school decile rankings of 2nd for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades (far below the averages around 5th or 6th decile)—and concluded with a 99.2% predicted probability that you would fail the bar exam. This extremely high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of strongest influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your family's upper-middle income bracket. Your low Year 3 decile was the most powerful driver, as it strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar failure compared to students who ranked higher in their classes. Similarly, your Year 1 decile pulled the prediction further toward failure, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often persist. Your family's upper-middle income also contributed toward the failure prediction, likely because the model associates that bracket with patterns of underperformance in bar passage among similar students. While your plan to attend full-time (the norm for 93% of students) didn't sway things much, these top factors overshadowed positives like your LSAT score. Compared to typical students who get approved, your decile rankings are in the bottom tiers, where failure rates skyrocket, unlike the mid-range performers who pass more reliably. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim above 36.7, building a stronger academic record through postgraduate courses or a master's program to boost your deciles, and gathering letters from mentors who can highlight your potential beyond these metrics. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours.
